items.title: Risk appetite among investors is skyrocketing: Risky asset fund inflows have exceeded safe asset fund inflows by a record $220 billion over the last 4...
items.description_html:
Risk appetite among investors is skyrocketing:

Risky asset fund inflows have exceeded safe asset fund inflows by a record $220 billion over the last 4 weeks.

Risky assets represent equities and corporate bonds, among others, while safe assets include money markets and Treasury bonds.

This stands in sharp contrast to 2025, when safe asset funds drew more inflows than risky funds for most of the year.

The recent surge even surpasses the highs of ~$200 billion seen during the 2021 meme stock frenzy.

To put this into perspective, during the 2020 pandemic, safe asset fund inflows exceeded risky fund inflows by more than $500 billion.

Investors are taking on more risk than ever.

Folks! Don't judge AI based on what you chat! The free versions of these chats are explicitly stripped off to save on token costs. If you don't want to pay, it's fine. But don't judge based on chat responses.
What matters:
Context Engineering (very diff from prompt engineering, go figure)
RAG & Various newer versions of it
Knowledge Graphs
MCP/Connectors/Protocols
Tooling/Function Calling
Finally, there is always fine tuning as a last resort for complex domains

move beyond chatting, build something for once.

items.description_html:
Rising aspirations. Rising prices. A consumer who refuses to settle. Memory costs have surged, pushing device prices higher. Yet demand for high-spec smartphones stays strong across every segment. EMI has become the bridge between the two.

@Flipkart has seen this firsthand across 140 million smartphone buyers. More Indians are choosing longer tenures to access devices they truly want, rather than settling for what fits the budget upfront.

@CounterpointTR's Smartphone Insights Report 2026 captures exactly how this dynamic is reshaping buying behavior.

Interesting read.. https://www.storyboard18.com/amp/how-it-works/ai-features-shape-buying-decisions-for-89-of-smartphone-users-in-india-report-finds-96889.html

items.title: E-Commerce M&A is surging: E-Commerce sector M&A activity rose +12.8% YoY in 2025, to 97 transactions, posting the best year since 2021. Meanwhile, th...
items.description_html:
E-Commerce M&A is surging:

E-Commerce sector M&A activity rose +12.8% YoY in 2025, to 97 transactions, posting the best year since 2021.

Meanwhile, the 2025 holiday season, November 1st to December 31st, saw dollars spent online jump +6.8% YoY to a record $257.8 billion.

Even as consumer savings have dwindled, E-Commerce sales continue to post records.

How is this possible?

Americans spent a record $20 billion on "Buy Now, Pay Later" services in the holiday season alone.

Despite inflation and rising debt levels, consumers simply keep on spending.

Keep watching E-Commerce.

items.description_html:
April was a historic month for US stocks:

The Nasdaq Composite rallied +15.3% in April, its best monthly performance since April 2020.

The S&P 500 gained +10.4%, its biggest monthly gain since November 2020.

This also marks the 3rd-best month over the last 15 years.

This comes as Alphabet, $GOOGL, surged +33.8%, its best month since October 2004, the year the firm went public.

Furthermore, Intel, $INTC, gained +114.1%, the strongest month in the firm’s 55-year history.

Tech giants are getting even bigger.

items.title: RKFORGE Q4 FY26 showed strong operational recovery with 28.5% YoY revenue growth and 671 bps margin expansion, though the company missed all key FY26 ...
items.description_html:
RKFORGE Q4 FY26 showed strong operational recovery with 28.5% YoY revenue growth and 671 bps margin expansion, though the company missed all key FY26 guidance targets.

Normalized PAT swung from loss to profit, and operating cash flow improved dramatically. However, consistent guidance misses, subsidiary drag, and elevated capex limit the alpha potential. The strong order book and FY27 capex moderation provide some forward visibility.

Positive Factors

Q4 revenue grew 28.46% YoY to ₹1,216.8 Cr, showing strong recovery momentum

Q4 EBITDA margin expanded 671 bps YoY to 17.11% driven by operating leverage

Normalized PAT swung from loss of ₹23.6 Cr to profit of ₹48.5 Cr quarter-over-quarter

Operating cash flow improved dramatically to ₹840 Cr in FY26 vs ₹33.5 Cr in FY25

Working capital improved significantly with trade receivables decreasing ₹2,114 Cr

Order book of ₹6,000-7,000 Cr provides strong revenue visibility for FY28

Risk Factors

Missed all key FY26 guidance: revenue (7.4% vs double-digit), EBITDA margin (17.17% vs 21-22%), net debt (₹2,169 Cr vs <₹2,000 Cr), capex (₹710 Cr vs ₹415-465 Cr)

Finance costs rose 26.8% YoY to ₹21,034.5 Lakh driven by higher debt for capex

Subsidiaries and JV created ₹1,470.8 Lakh drag on consolidated PAT, mainly from Rail Wheel JV loss of ₹1,785.07 Lakh

Free cash flow remained negative at ₹839.3 Cr due to heavy capex spending

Management credibility at risk given consistent guidance misses in FY26

items.title: Punjab Chemicals delivered strong FY26 PAT growth of 64.3% to ₹63.96 crore, driven by margin expansion and improved cash generation. Q4 EBITDA margin...
items.description_html:
Punjab Chemicals delivered strong FY26 PAT growth of 64.3% to ₹63.96 crore, driven by margin expansion and improved cash generation. Q4 EBITDA margin of 13.2% exceeded management's normalized run rate of 12%, though full-year revenue (14.4% YoY) and margin (11.3%) slightly missed guidance.

The company generated strong free cash flow of ₹39.80 crore and reduced net debt-to-equity to 0.32x. However, Q4 saw a 15.4% QoQ revenue decline and significant inventory build-up, indicating near-term headwinds from seasonality and market conditions.

Positive Factors

PAT grew 64.3% YoY to ₹63.96 crore, driven by margin expansion and lower tax rate

Operating cash flow was 1.34x PAT at ₹85.54 crore, indicating high-quality earnings

Free cash flow turned positive at ₹39.80 crore vs negative ₹8.39 crore in FY25

Q4 EBITDA margin expanded to 13.2% from 10.5% YoY, a 261 bps improvement

Net debt-to-equity improved to 0.32x from 0.39x, with net debt reducing 7.1% YoY

Cost of materials grew only 5.9% YoY vs 14.4% revenue growth, showing cost management excellence

Risk Factors

Q4 revenue declined 15.4% QoQ due to seasonality and planned shutdown at Derabassi block

FY26 revenue growth of 14.4% slightly missed management guidance of 15-20%

FY26 EBITDA margin of 11.3% slightly missed guidance of 11.5-12.5%

Q4 inventory build-up of ₹49.89 crore was substantial, raising working capital concerns

Export demand remains subdued per management commentary

Lalru capacity utilization was running at 60% in Q3 vs 80% target

“Our view is that the per capita consumption of tokens in India will increase dramatically. ChatGPT and Google Gemini are reaching the top 100 million Indians and often through free products. We need to be able to think about 1.4 billion people using a lot of tokens. That will come from the large banks in the country, the large insurance firms, the large education institutions all providing AI to their respective users and the government providing government-to-citizen services.”

items.title: SIS delivered strong Q4 FY26 with 31% YoY revenue growth and Operating PAT growth of 27.9%, driven by Security Solutions segments. The company success...
items.description_html:
SIS delivered strong Q4 FY26 with 31% YoY revenue growth and Operating PAT growth of 27.9%, driven by Security Solutions segments. The company successfully transitioned from loss to profit with PAT of ₹102.5 crore. Facility Management segment showed margin expansion while Security segments faced margin pressure.

Cash conversion was excellent at 107% and leverage improved significantly. However, cost growth outpacing revenue in employee benefits and margin compression in security segments remain concerns.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 31.0% YoY to ₹4,489.30 crore

Operating PAT grew 27.9% YoY to ₹105.5 crore

Facility Management margin expanded 80 bps YoY to 5.5%

Net Debt/EBITDA improved to 0.99x from 1.25x

CFO/EBITDA conversion was strong at 107%

DSO improved to 63 days from 65 days YoY

International Security grew 36.9% YoY (17.1% constant currency)

Risk Factors

Employee benefits expense grew 33.1% YoY, outpacing revenue growth of 31.0%

Security Services India margin contracted 50 bps YoY to 5.1%

Security Services International margin contracted 20 bps YoY to 3.8%

Overall EBITDA margin contracted 20 bps YoY to 4.61%

Depreciation increased 83.4% YoY due to capex and new ROU assets

Net debt increased 58.4% YoY to ₹748.87 crore

items.description_html:
Sudarshan Pharma delivered a major revenue surprise in FY26 with 39.2% growth vs 15% guidance, driven by strong specialty chemicals demand and vertical integration push.

However, margin compression and negative operating cash flow for two consecutive years highlight execution challenges. The aggressive capex and acquisition strategy positions the company for growth but increases financial risk with D/E now at 1.81x.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 39.2% YoY to Rs 703.07 Cr, beating management guidance of 15% growth by 24.2 percentage points

PAT grew 55.7% YoY to Rs 23.30 Cr, outpacing revenue growth indicating operating leverage

EPS grew 56.5% YoY to Rs 0.97, demonstrating earnings acceleration

Q4 revenue grew 36.7% YoY with 31.5% sequential growth, showing strong momentum

Acquired API manufacturing facility from Srogen Lifesciences for Rs 25.50 Cr, advancing vertical integration strategy

Non-current assets grew 92.2% YoY with 150.6% increase in PPE, reflecting aggressive capex for capacity expansion

Risk Factors

EBITDA margin contracted 43 bps to 7.30% in FY26, missing margin improvement trajectory toward 12.9% target

Operating cash flow negative for second consecutive year at Rs -2.30 Cr, indicating poor cash conversion

Working capital investment of Rs 51.27 Cr consumed entire operating profit, inventory and receivable days increased significantly

Debt-to-equity ratio deteriorated from 1.30x to 1.81x, increasing financial risk

Cost of materials consumed grew 337% YoY, indicating raw material cost pressure or business mix shift to manufacturing

Finance costs increased 60.2% YoY to Rs 25.70 Cr, reflecting higher borrowings to fund expansion

items.title: While his approach isn't entirely wrong & we may not necessarily be able to lead in this race, owning enough of the stack for resilient autonomy is an...
items.description_html:
While his approach isn't entirely wrong & we may not necessarily be able to lead in this race, owning enough of the stack for resilient autonomy is an absolute, uncompromising necessity.

> Own critical pieces of digital infra such as identity management
> Sovereign fine tuned models
> Domestic compute build up

In the age of geopolitical instability, not having foundational autonomy is very risky. Having indigenous alternatives to critical parts of digital stack is a necessity. Controlling, protecting those parts is even higher necessity.

During WW2, U.S. didn't drop a nuke to destroy, they did to make a statement. Back then only 1 country had it. What's the parallel today? It's AI & what can be controlled with it. During galwan valley clash, Chinese hackers did manage to plant a malware, India's power grid infra was targetted. RLDC, SLDCs were targetted. NSE faced 40cr+ cyber attacks in a single day during Sindoor.

Bottom line, India needs to build autonomy as much as possible. It's not to win the race but to build strong resilient future.


Nandan Nilekani: India doesn't need to lead the world in building the most advanced AI models. But it must lead in ensuring benefits of AI are widely shared.

@rvenk and I have an op-ed in The @EconomicTimes

https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/opinion/et-commentary/its-popular-mechanics-101-indias-edge-lies-in-using-ai-to-enhance-human-productivity-not-replace-it/articleshow/130586820.cms

items.title: RT CSEINDIA: Today, CSE launches “Let’s Be Water Wise in a Climate-Risked World” 💧🌍 A campaign to bring water back to the centre of public di...
items.description_html:
RT CSEINDIA
Today, CSE launches “Let’s Be Water Wise in a Climate-Risked World” 💧🌍

A campaign to bring water back to the centre of public discourse and action.

We begin with a 37-second film we created over two decades ago, directed by @nanditadas. It carries a simple, poetic, and urgent idea: Catch water where it falls.

This public service film helped spark a global conversation around rainwater harvesting, earning international recognition including:

Grand Prix – Environfilms (2006)
Best Short Film – FICA, Brazil (2005)
Best Short Film – Torino Film Festival, Czech Republic

Why revisit it now?

Because what was once a solution is now a necessity. With 2026 expected to be shaped by a ‘super’ El Niño, water stress is no longer a distant risk; it is unfolding around us.

Over the coming weeks, we will bring you:
▶️ Ground-level reportage and Down To Earth stories
▶️ Data-driven infographics and interactives
▶️ A curated bouquet of films, starting with this award-winning classic.

All anchored in a principle CSE has long championed:
Capture, conserve and reuse water— starting where it falls.

🎬 Watch the film that started the conversation.

@down2earthindia | @sunitanar

items.title: RT Pune Mirror: Following a bird flu outbreak in the Navapur area of Nandurbar, Maharashtra, the local administration has ordered the massive culling ...
items.description_html:
RT Pune Mirror
Following a bird flu outbreak in the Navapur area of Nandurbar, Maharashtra, the local administration has ordered the massive culling of approximately 1.5 lakh chickens. This strict emergency measure was initiated after samples collected from three local poultry farms tested positive at a laboratory in Indore. To prevent the disease from spreading further, authorities have mandated culling all chickens within a 1-kilometer radius of the infected farms, alongside declaring a 3-kilometer containment zone and a 5-kilometer buffer zone. Officials have formed a special committee to inspect all poultry farms in the taluka and have assured financial compensation for the affected farmers who suffer losses.

#Birdflu #Maharashtra #latestnews

items.title: RT MacroSpectrum: The AI layoff trap- There or not? https://macro-spectrum.com/opinion/the-ai-layoff-trap-there-or-not 1. In this 16 page analysis we ...
items.description_html:
RT MacroSpectrum
The AI layoff trap- There or not?

https://macro-spectrum.com/opinion/the-ai-layoff-trap-there-or-not

1. In this 16 page analysis we look at two of the most ground breaking research papers released in early March'26.

2. The first one titled "The AI Layoff Trap" is a working paper written by Brett Hemenway Falk (University of Pennsylvania) and Gerry Tsoukalas (Boston University) which showcases a formal economic model to explain why competitive firms will collectively over-automate using AI even when every firm can see the harm it causes and why most proposed policy remedies fail to stop it.

3. The paper begins with a paradox. Rational, forward-looking firms should recognize that displacing workers also destroys their own customer base. Displaced workers are consumers, and when their income is not replaced, aggregate demand falls hurting all firms. Yet firms are racing to automate anyway. The paper asks: if the cliff is visible to everyone, why do firms race toward it?

4. We then look at another research paper released by Anthropic “Labor market impacts of AI: A new measure and early evidence” written by Maxim Massenkoff and Peter McCrory.

5. The paper acknowledges that AI's rapid spread has sparked a wave of labor market research but stays cautions that past forecasting attempts have often been wrong. The authors argue that studying AI's effects before major disruptions occur — rather than in hindsight — gives researchers the best chance of accurately identifying any future economic harm.

6. Most existing research measures theoretical AI exposure — what an LLM could do. This paper introduces a new metric called Observed Exposure. The key insight is that theoretical capability and actual deployment are very different things. A task might be theoretically automatable but still not show up in real usage due to legal constraints, software requirements, human verification needs, or simple inertia.

7. The paper finds no current evidence of AI-driven unemployment, but identifies a tentative early signal that hiring of young workers into exposed occupations has slowed.

8. Our own Take: We believe employment loss due to AI is real, but extent can be debated ad infinitum. Automation has already eliminated or reduced roles in manufacturing, data entry, customer service, and routine white-collar work. AI accelerates this.

9. Past technological revolutions (steam, electricity, computers) played out over generations. AI may move faster than workers can retrain. Unlike earlier automation, AI threatens lawyers, radiologists, coders, and writers — not just manual workers.

10. Even when jobs aren't eliminated, AI can reduce bargaining power and suppress wages. Workers in routine, repetitive roles such as call centers, data processing, some legal/financial tasks face real disruption now. The biggest risk isn't permanent mass unemployment- it's a painful transition period where displaced workers don't have the skills or support to move into new roles quickly enough.

11. So as a policy response retraining, educational budget allocations towards AI training as well as wider social safety nets become essential to reduce the employment loss impact on economic activity.

12. From a market perspective, above implies higher fiscal deficits. Corporates adopting AI exclusively will see higher profits at the cost of government balancesheets in case social security net widens. As a consequence, can the Mag-7 valuations still go higher with higher bond yields specially long end yields. In another soon to be out report., we cover the relationship between corporate profitability, government's fiscal deficit, higher bond yields and the breaking point where this causality breaks due to social unrest.

items.description_html:
RT CSEINDIA
When extreme weather disrupts elections, it disrupts democracy.

Floods and storms account for nearly 67% of election disruptions recorded globally over the past two decades. Heatwaves add another 9%.

As assembly elections began in India, an intense heatwave across several regions brought a key issue into focus: how extreme weather can affect voter turnout and participation.

A new report by the @Int_IDEA found that extreme weather has affected at least 94 elections across 52 countries in the last 20 years.

In this video, @down2earthindia looks at how climate change is increasingly disrupting elections, and what that means for the future of democratic participation. Watch now: https://youtu.be/RBjldAdtexk?si=3SWVZL8eRtBJXGQy

items.title: RT Parimal: If Ratnam (I will write about him separately) was the Soul of the Swadeshi pen, Dr. Radhika Nath Saha was its Brain. He patented the tech ...
items.description_html:
RT Parimal
If Ratnam (I will write about him separately) was the Soul of the Swadeshi pen, Dr. Radhika Nath Saha was its Brain. He patented the tech in 4 countries before the rest of India even knew the game had started.

The reason the world knows Ratnam & not Saha is a classic case of Iconic vs. Technical branding. Ratnam had the Gandhi Endorsement. In a nationalist movement, a letter from the Mahatma is worth more than 14 patents in Washington. Dr. Saha was a Pure Scientist. He operated in the Pre-Gandhi Swadeshi era (1905-1910). When he died in 1933, his factory, Luxmi Stylo-Pen Works, lacked the political branding that later defined the Ratnam legacy.

Dr. Radhika Nath Saha was a medical doctor by profession, serving in the British Indian medical services. He became obsessed with fountain pens because, as a traveling doctor, carrying inkpots & quills was a logistical nightmare. He realized the pens of the early 1900s were primitive gravity drippers. He used his knowledge of Human Circulatory Systems (veins & valves) to rethink how ink should move. He essentially treated the pen as an Artificial Artery.

In 1905, while India was supposedly backward, Dr. Saha was filing patents in London, Berlin, & Washington D.C. 1 of his most famous patents was for a Stylo-Pen with a telescopic feed. He invented a system where the needle of the pen would retract & self-clean. This solved the #1 problem of the era: Ink Coagulation.

The British govt, despite their colonial bias, had to admit in their official reports that Dr. Saha’s Luxmi Stylo-Pen was superior to many European imports. He traveled to Germany & England to source the most advanced Vulcanite & Iridium tipping material. He refused to let the Europeans assemble them. He brought the machinery to Banares & trained local artisans to work with a precision of 0.01 mm.

He wrote a massive treatise titled Romance of Pen Industries (attached the pdf for reading), which is arguably the world's 1st comprehensive technical manual on writing instruments. It was so detailed that it terrified the British Waterman & Parker companies. Dr. Saha’s pens were so well-engineered that they were used by the British Secret Service & high-ranking officials in the Cantonment offices, who had no idea they were using a product designed by a man who wanted to end their rule through intellectual dominance.

Book Link - https://ia800103.us.archive.org/28/items/in.ernet.dli.2015.92332/2015.92332.Romance-Of-Pen-Industries.pdf

items.title: The most widely used indicator to understand how risky pregnancy and childbirth are in a region is the maternal mortality ratio (MMR). MMR is the numb...
items.description_html:
The most widely used indicator to understand how risky pregnancy and childbirth are in a region is the maternal mortality ratio (MMR). MMR is the number of maternal deaths, during pregnancy or within 42 days after, for every 100,000 live births.

Maternal mortality in India stood at 398 deaths for every 100,000 live births in 1998, and by 2023, this number had decreased to 88 maternal deaths per 100,000 live births.

Improving access to healthcare has been a key factor in this decline. A study found that nearly half of maternal deaths occurred at home, with 14% happening in transit between home and a health facility.

The number of institutional births in India has risen significantly over the last twenty years, lowering the risk of maternal mortality. In 1993, only a quarter of deliveries took place in public or private hospitals; by 2021, this had increased to 89%.

Even with this progress, over a quarter of mothers in India’s poorest rural households continue to give birth at home. Five of 36 states - Arunachal Pradesh, Bihar, Jharkhand, Meghalaya and Nagaland - still report over one in five deliveries at home.

Alongside institutional births, access to adequate healthcare during pregnancy is crucial for reducing risks before delivery. How does this access look across India? Read @nileena_suresh’s piece to find out: https://www.dataforindia.com/maternal-mortality/?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=post

#Hospital #Birth #Death #Health #India #DataForIndia

items.title: American consumer confidence is trending lower among most generations: The 6-month average of the Consumer Confidence Index for Generation X is down t...
items.description_html:
American consumer confidence is trending lower among most generations:

The 6-month average of the Consumer Confidence Index for Generation X is down to ~78 points, the lowest in at least 4.5 years.

At the same time, the gauge for Baby Boomers is down to ~83 points, the lowest since at least October 2021.

Confidence readings for both age groups have been in a steady decline since early 2025.

Similarly, the Silent Generation's Consumer Confidence Index is down to ~91 points, the weakest level in at least 4.5 years.

Generation Z remains below its 2021-2025 range, despite rising slightly in April to ~110 points.

Consumer confidence continues to erode.

items.title: "My father had told me to never come begging for money. I had to earn money to invest it. The only way I could have raised capital was by trading,’ R...
items.description_html:
"My father had told me to never come begging for money. I had to earn money to invest it. The only way I could have raised capital was by trading,’ Rakesh Jhunjhunwala would often say.

Sesa Goa, an iron-ore mining company, was his first trading bet. The company is now part of Vedanta Ltd. "There was this fellow in the stock market, Mr Bajaj, who would keep talking about Sesa Goa. He would catch people to convince them about it. Nobody would listen. When he explained the logic to me, I found it attractive," he said.

By this time, he had accumulated a capital of Rs 50–55 lakh. He bought Sesa Goa shares worth Rs 1 Cr using forward trading (a type of leveraged or debt-fuelled trade).

According to Rakesh Sir, the price of iron ore was set in advance, and it was going to be 26% higher in the next year. The company would also benefit from rupee depreciation. He bought 3 lakh shares in Sesa Goa.

"The price went from 26 to 65 in three months and to 2200 in three years. That’s how I made the initial money," he said in a lecture at FLAME University in 2008. Rakesh’s capital rose to Rs 2–2.5 crore by the early 1990s.

Book SRC – The Big Bull of Dalal Street; no stock reco

items.title: AI is reshaping the American workplace. 66.3% of US workers earning over $200,000 used AI tools at work over the last 12 months, according to a Fed su...
items.description_html:
AI is reshaping the American workplace.

66.3% of US workers earning over $200,000 used AI tools at work over the last 12 months, according to a Fed survey.

This is followed by those earning $100,000-$200,000 and $50,000-$100,000, at 51.6% and 40.2%, respectively.

By comparison, just 15.9% of Americans earning less than $50,000 used AI tools at work over the same period.

Furthermore, college graduates were more than twice as likely to use AI at work as those without a degree, at 58.7% versus 22.9%.

Full-time workers used AI at nearly 2 times the rate of part-time workers, at 42.7% versus 24.7%.

Among workers who do use AI, 68.0% said it makes their job easier, and 56.7% claimed it boosts their productivity.

AI is transforming how Americans work.

items.title: A growing number of Americans can no longer afford their car loans: A record 42.6% of underwater car buyers turned to an 84-month (7-year) loan to kee...
items.description_html:
A growing number of Americans can no longer afford their car loans:

A record 42.6% of underwater car buyers turned to an 84-month (7-year) loan to keep monthly payments manageable in Q1 2026.

Being underwater means owing more on a car loan than the car is currently worth, resulting in negative equity.

Rather than paying off the difference, many buyers roll this gap into their next loan, pushing themselves deeper into debt.

This percentage has DOUBLED since 2016 and is on track for its 3rd consecutive annual increase.

Buyers with negative equity financed an average of ~$56,000 for a new car in Q1 2026, ~$12,000 more than the average new vehicle buyer.

As a result, their average monthly payment rose to $932, the highest on record.

In March, car loan default rates jumped to their highest since 2010.

Auto-loan distress is surging.

items.title: One thing that is really changing is how AI is an enabler to disrupt even low margin industries with lower ROCE , the typical argument is that competi...
items.description_html:
One thing that is really changing is how AI is an enabler to disrupt even low margin industries with lower ROCE , the typical argument is that competition that is tech and process enabled with loads of capital , enters high ROCE industries to change the capital structure of the whole industry causing incremental capital deployed to fetch lower returns

But with AI , we can see even lower margin industries feel the heat as lower margin industries typically are price linked or cycle linked , now with analytical tools available at democratic scale , a 2 billion $ market cap company and 100 million $ cap company will have access to similar tools with predictive analytics rather than reactive analytics , to defend their margin , and the only edge will remain large capital, but that makes you weak at points and less agile

I recently read this somewhere , it is never too late to disrupt an industry ! Don't think it is just the cream at risk the risk might be deeper at the bottom end of industrial margins!

items.title: The US is draining its oil reserves at a rapid pace: The Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) dropped -7.12 million barrels last week, the largest weekly...
items.description_html:
The US is draining its oil reserves at a rapid pace:

The Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) dropped -7.12 million barrels last week, the largest weekly drawdown since October 2022.

This marks the 5th consecutive weekly decline, the longest streak since 2023.

Over this period, US oil reserves in the SPR have fallen -17 million barrels, to 398 million, the lowest since April 2025.

This comes as total US oil and fuel exports surpassed 14 million barrels per day for the first time in history, as overseas buyers scramble to replace lost Middle Eastern supply.

Furthermore, commercial crude stocks declined -6.23 million barrels last week, the largest weekly decline since early February.

Gasoline and distillate stocks also fell sharply, by -6.08 million barrels and -4.49 million barrels, respectively, pushing total gasoline supplies to their lowest seasonal level since 2014.

Global demand for US oil has never been higher.

items.title: Godrej Agrovet FY26 results showed significant guidance misses on revenue (9.1% vs 16-18% target) and profitability (EBITDA +0.4% vs 16-18% target). T...
items.description_html:
Godrej Agrovet FY26 results showed significant guidance misses on revenue (9.1% vs 16-18% target) and profitability (EBITDA +0.4% vs 16-18% target). The Vegetable Oil segment was a standout performer with 39% revenue growth and 67% PBIT growth, offsetting weakness in Crop Care (margin miss from 30% guidance to 15.1%) and Dairy (PBIT down 54%).

Strong cash conversion (2.88x CFO/PAT) provides financial flexibility but increased leverage (0.68x debt/equity) and margin compression are concerns. The guidance miss indicates execution challenges rather than alpha opportunity.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 9.06% YoY to ₹10,232.68 Cr for FY26

Vegetable Oil segment revenue grew 39.43% YoY with PBIT margin expanding to 19.32% from 16.15%

Operating cash flow strong at ₹1,281.17 Cr, up 32.2% YoY with CFO/PAT at 2.88x

Animal Nutrition segment PBIT margin improved to 7.03% from 6.09%

Interest service coverage ratio improved to 5.36x from 4.75x

Poultry segment profitability improved with PBIT growing 5.5% YoY and margins expanding to 4.2% from 3.7%

Risk Factors

Revenue guidance of 16-18% missed with actual 9.1% growth

Profitability guidance of 16-18% missed significantly with EBITDA up only 0.4%

Crop Care segment PBIT margin collapsed to 15.1% from 17.7% guidance of ~30%

Dairy segment PBIT declined 54% YoY with margin collapsing to 1.2% from 2.7%

EBITDA margin contracted by 72 bps to 8.53% for full year

Debt-to-equity ratio increased to 0.68x from 0.49x indicating higher leverage

Current ratio declined to 0.95x from 0.99x indicating tighter liquidity

items.title: ACC reported strong 23% revenue growth for FY26 but profitability collapsed with 58% PBT decline in Q4 and 495 bps margin compression. The Ready Mix C...
items.description_html:
ACC reported strong 23% revenue growth for FY26 but profitability collapsed with 58% PBT decline in Q4 and 495 bps margin compression. The Ready Mix Concrete segment is a clear bright spot with 36.7% revenue growth and 581 bps margin expansion, emerging as a new growth driver.

However, the core cement business suffered severe margin erosion (539 bps) due to cost pressures not being passed through. Cash conversion deteriorated significantly with negative operating cash flow of -₹1,364 Cr and working capital stress evident from 230% receivables growth. The amalgamation with Ambuja Cements remains pending regulatory approval.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 23% YoY to ₹25,761.70 Cr in FY26, meeting double-digit growth target

Ready Mix Concrete segment revenue grew 36.7% YoY in Q4 and 40% YoY for full year

RMC segment EBIT margins expanded 581 bps YoY to 10.85% in Q4

RMC segment EBIT grew 194% YoY in Q4 to ₹62.24 Cr

Net worth grew 10.8% YoY to ₹20,554.49 Cr, balance sheet remains strong

Dividend of ₹7.50 per share maintained for FY26

Risk Factors

Q4 PBT declined 58% YoY due to expenses growing 22.7% vs revenue 18%

EBITDA margin contracted 495 bps YoY to 8.83% in Q4

Cement segment EBIT margins crashed 539 bps YoY to 4.57%

Operating cash flow negative at -₹1,364 Cr vs +₹1,711 Cr in FY25

Working capital stress: Trade receivables up 230% YoY, cash down 49%

Free cash flow deeply negative at -₹2,791 Cr

FY26 capex of ₹1,427 Cr far below ₹10,000 Cr guidance

CCI penalty contingency of ₹1,147.59 Cr pending Supreme Court

items.title: NSDL delivered solid FY26 results with revenue growing 7.73% YoY and PAT up 10.75%. The core depository business remains highly profitable with 49.5% ...
items.description_html:
NSDL delivered solid FY26 results with revenue growing 7.73% YoY and PAT up 10.75%. The core depository business remains highly profitable with 49.5% EBIT margin and generates 95% of group profits.

The Payments Bank business has turned profitable (₹2,056 Lakh vs ₹373 Lakh) with strong revenue momentum (+49% YoY), though profitability remains thin at 2.75% margin. Market share gains in incremental BO accounts (15.89% vs 8.84%) demonstrate competitive strength. Key risk is the Database segment facing headwinds from ICEGATE migration (revenue -3.8% YoY, profit -34.2% YoY). Strong cash generation (1.88x CFO/PAT) supports the doubled dividend.

Positive Factors

Depository segment maintained 49.5% EBIT margin in FY26, generating 95% of group PAT on 46% of revenue

Banking Services segment revenue grew 55.9% QoQ and 49.2% YoY in Q4 FY26

Incremental market share of BO accounts jumped to 15.89% in FY26 from 8.84% in FY25

Banking segment turned profitable at EBIT level (₹2,056.56 Lakh vs ₹372.87 Lakh in FY25)

Operating cash flow strong at ₹71,348.58 Lakh with 1.88x CFO/PAT ratio

Free cash flow generation strong at ₹57,801.01 Lakh

Dividend doubled to ₹4.00 per share from ₹2.00

Risk Factors

Database Management Services revenue declined 3.8% YoY and profit fell 34.2% YoY due to ICEGATE migration risk

Q4 FY26 expenses surged 38.3% QoQ outpacing revenue growth of 27.4% QoQ

Banking Services EBIT margin very low at 2.75% despite revenue contribution of 53.9%

Employee costs rose 30.5% YoY to ₹18,077.65 Lakh

Finance costs up 66.5% YoY and D&A up 35.7% YoY indicating increasing cost base

items.title: Indus Towers delivered solid operational performance in FY26 with normalized EBITDA growing 12.9% and PAT rising 14.0% YoY after adjusting for one-tim...
items.description_html:
Indus Towers delivered solid operational performance in FY26 with normalized EBITDA growing 12.9% and PAT rising 14.0% YoY after adjusting for one-time provision reversals. Margin expanded 250 bps to 56.0% demonstrating operational leverage.

The balance sheet strengthened significantly with debt reduced 59.3% and net cash position of Rs 50,058 Mn. However, reported PAT declined 28.1% due to high base effect. The company resumed dividends of Rs 14/share and continues aggressive capex of Rs 86,258 Mn for network expansion. No explicit guidance beats identified as no specific prior management guidance numbers were provided for comparison.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 7.9% YoY to Rs 324,931 Mn for FY26

Normalized EBITDA grew 12.9% YoY to Rs 181,831 Mn with 250 bps margin expansion

Normalized PAT grew 14.0% YoY to Rs 69,443 Mn

Borrowings reduced by 59.3% YoY to Rs 9,209 Mn,
achieving net cash position of Rs 50,058 Mn

Operating cash flow to PAT ratio strong at 2.19x

Free cash flow of Rs 70,582 Mn generated in FY26
Dividend of Rs 14 per share resumed

Power costs grew slower than revenue (4.8% vs 7.9%), showing energy efficiency

Risk Factors

Reported PAT declined 28.1% YoY due to high base from provision write-back in FY25

Total expenses surged 56.5% YoY for FY26 due to prior year reversal

Customer concentration risk with significant revenue from single large customer (Vodafone Idea)

High capex of Rs 86,258 Mn limits free cash flow available for distribution

Cash and cash equivalents declined 68.4% YoY to Rs 473 Mn

items.description_html:
BREAKING: The European Central Bank is likely to raise interest rates at their June meeting unless there are "positive developments" on energy prices and ending the Iran War, per Bloomberg.

Details include:

1. Should the Iran War persist, there is only "a very small chance" that rate hikes can be avoided

2. Headline inflation in Europe has surged back above 3%

3. Fears of stagflation are growing as Q1 2026 growth in the euro zone was just 0.1%

4. The IEA has called the current situation the "biggest energy security threat in history"

Europe is about to experience a major shift in policy.

Crypto card spending volume has surged +500% since September 2024, now running at $600 million per month.

As a result, stablecoin-linked payment cards are now one of the fastest growing businesses on the blockchain, with 90% of transactions captured by Visa, $V.

Visa's strategy has centered around partnering with emerging infrastructure providers which reduces reliance on traditional sponsor banks.

The growth comes amid the launch of Jupiter Global which returns 4-10% cash back to crypto cards and has seen +660% MoM volume growth in April.

Crypto card adoption is growing.

items.title: EVEREADY delivered a solid Q4 with 9.4% YoY revenue growth and 32.7% YoY growth in normalized PAT. The balance sheet transformed significantly with 38...
items.description_html:
EVEREADY delivered a solid Q4 with 9.4% YoY revenue growth and 32.7% YoY growth in normalized PAT. The balance sheet transformed significantly with 38% debt reduction and D/E ratio improving to 0.29x.

Alkaline market share accelerated to ~19% from 14% over four quarters. However, EBITDA margin of 8.56% remains below the 10% target, and achieving the FY27 revenue target of ₹1,800 crore requires a steep 23.7% growth acceleration - a stretch from current 8.2% growth rate.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 9.4% YoY to ₹327.23 crore in Q4 FY26

Normalized PAT grew 32.7% YoY to ₹12.06 crore
(excluding exceptional items and tax credit)

Total debt reduced by 37.8% to ₹179.37 crore, D/E ratio improved to 0.29x from 0.63x

Working capital at 14.25% of revenue, meeting management target of <15%

Alkaline market share increased from 14% to ~19% over four quarters

Sixth consecutive quarter of YoY revenue growth confirmed

Operating leverage visible with PBT growth (32.7%) significantly outpacing revenue growth (9.4%)

Asset sale of Noida land generated ₹105.20 crore exceptional gain

Risk Factors

EBITDA margin at 8.56%, below management target of 10%

Gross margin compressed to 43.4% from 45.4% in FY25 indicating pricing pressure

Operating cash flow declined to ₹83.36 crore from ₹129.67 crore in FY25

Revenue declined 10.9% QoQ reflecting seasonal patterns

Need 23.7% YoY growth to achieve ₹1,800 crore

revenue target in FY27 - significant acceleration from 8.2% in FY26

FY26 revenue of ₹1,455.39 crore is 19% below FY27 target of ₹1,800 crore

items.title: IndiaMART reported 13.9% YoY revenue growth but faced significant margin compression of 393 bps as expenses grew 19% vs revenue 13.9%. The 72.2% PAT d...
items.description_html:
IndiaMART reported 13.9% YoY revenue growth but faced significant margin compression of 393 bps as expenses grew 19% vs revenue 13.9%. The 72.2% PAT decline was primarily driven by a INR 1,427 Mn swing in other income (fair value losses on investments).

Normalized operating performance shows minimal growth (0.3% YoY), indicating the company is in an investment phase as guided. The Accounting Software segment is emerging as a growth driver with 83% revenue growth and narrowing losses. Strong cash conversion (1.46x CFO/PAT) and growing contract liabilities (17.1% YoY) provide some comfort for future revenue visibility.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 13.9% YoY to INR 4,043 Cr in Q4 FY26
Accounting Software segment revenue grew 83.2%
YoY with losses narrowing 70.8%

Contract liabilities grew 17.1% YoY to INR 19,653 Mn indicating strong revenue visibility

Operating cash flow strong at INR 6,942 Mn with CFO/PAT ratio of 1.46x

Dividend of INR 60 per share declared including special dividend

Core segment EBITDA margin at 36.2% though compressed YoY

Risk Factors

EBITDA margin contracted 393 bps YoY to 32.8% in Q4 due to cost growth outpacing revenue

Other income swung to loss of INR 339 Mn from INR 1,088 Mn, causing PAT decline of 72.2% YoY

Normalized PAT grew only 0.3% YoY showing minimal operational profit growth

Total expenses grew 19.0% YoY vs revenue growth of 13.9%, indicating operating deleverage

Core Web Services segment profit stalled at +0.4% YoY with margin compression of 339 bps

Q3 operational KPIs showed paying suppliers declined sequentially and active buyers down 2% YoY

items.title: Equitas Small Finance Bank delivered a strong Q4 FY26 with PAT growing 405% YoY, driven by 52% provision decline and successful microfinance stress no...
items.description_html:
Equitas Small Finance Bank delivered a strong Q4 FY26 with PAT growing 405% YoY, driven by 52% provision decline and successful microfinance stress normalization.

The bank beat guidance on advances growth (18.1% vs mid-teen target), credit cost (1.18% vs <1.5% target), and met universal bank license criteria (GNPA 2.60%, NNPA 0.72%). Key concern is deposit growth lagging advances, requiring 169% YoY increase in borrowings to fund loan growth.

Positive Factors

PAT grew 405.1% YoY to ₹21,268.29 Lakh in Q4 FY26

Retail Banking segment PBT turned positive at ₹23,813 Lakh vs ₹1,112 Lakh YoY - confirming microfinance recovery

Provisions declined 52% YoY to ₹12,410.95 Lakh, driving PBT surge

GNPA improved to 2.60% and NNPA to 0.72%, meeting universal bank license criteria

Advances grew 18.1% YoY exceeding mid-teen guidance

Credit cost of 1.18% came in below management's <1.5% guidance

PPOP grew 29.3% YoY reflecting strong core operating performance

NII grew 17.9% YoY indicating effective cost of funds management

Risk Factors

Deposit growth of 8.0% YoY lagged advances growth of 18.1%, necessitating 169% YoY increase in
borrowings

Operating cash flow negative at ₹(3,31,583.26) Lakh for full year FY26

Full-year normalized PAT declined 62.1% YoY due to high provisions in H1 FY26

C/I ratio at 67.75% still above management's target of 65% by Q4 FY27

Treasury segment revenue declined 11.9% YoY and PBT fell 32.9% YoY

items.title: Why CPUs are the next bottleneck? Demand for CPUs from AMD, Arm and Intel is spiking mainly because AI workloads are shifting from “GPU-only training...
items.description_html:
Why CPUs are the next bottleneck?

Demand for CPUs from AMD, Arm and Intel is spiking mainly because AI workloads are shifting from “GPU-only training” to AI agents and inference at scale that lean heavily on CPUs, creating a structural shortage and new pricing power.

Core reasons demand is exploding

AI data center build‑out: Cloud providers and enterprises are in an AI arms race, pouring billions into new data centers, which need racks of server CPUs alongside GPUs and custom accelerators. These AI-driven data centers are “swallowing” enormous numbers of high‑performance CPUs from AMD and Intel, and driving licensing demand for Arm’s architectures.

From training (GPU) to inference (CPU-heavy): Training large models is GPU‑dominated, but running them in production (inference), orchestrating requests, and managing memory and networking is far more CPU‑intensive. Intel’s recent earnings explicitly highlighted customers moving away from GPU‑only configurations toward setups where CPUs play a much larger role in AI inference and “agentic” systems.

Agentic AI and RAG need lots of “brains around the GPUs”: New “agentic” AI systems, where multiple AI agents coordinate tasks, plus retrieval‑augmented generation (RAG) pipelines, involve heavy scheduling, I/O, database calls and orchestration logic—all CPU‑bound activities. Analysts argue that these workloads can multiply CPU demand several‑fold because every GPU cluster needs a much fatter layer of host CPUs to avoid bottlenecks.

Why this hits AMD, Arm and Intel specifically
AMD (EPYC, x86 servers): AMD’s EPYC data center CPUs are direct beneficiaries of the surge in AI‑oriented server demand, with record data‑center revenue growth (over 30% YoY in late 2025) tied to AI and cloud build‑outs. As customers expand CPU footprints for AI inference and agents, analysts see a path for AMD to keep taking server share from Intel over time.

Intel (Xeon, data center CPU “comeback”): Intel just reported a sharp rebound in data center and AI CPU revenue (22% YoY), surprising the market and showing strong demand even amid supply constraints. Commentary from management emphasized that AI is moving “from foundational models to inference to agentic,” which requires more CPUs per GPU than older architectures, helping drive a “CPU renaissance.”

Arm (architectures for efficient CPUs): Arm does not sell CPUs directly in the same way, but licenses power‑efficient architectures used by hyperscalers (e.g., custom data center chips) and edge devices. As AI inference and agentic workloads move to energy‑sensitive environments (cloud, mobile, edge), Arm’s efficient designs become more valuable, boosting its licensing and royalty outlook, which investors are pricing into the stock.

Structural shortage and pricing power

Real CPU shortage: Reports now describe a genuine CPU crunch: demand from AI workloads and server refresh cycles is so strong that even lower‑tier Intel chips (“scrap” or downgraded dies) are selling at full price, which is unusual in normal cycles. PC and server OEMs like HP and Dell are already seeing a clear mismatch between CPU supply and demand, with rising costs and stretched lead times.

Price hikes and margin expansion: Intel and AMD have both moved to raise CPU prices roughly 10–15% in early 2026 for server and consumer products, something they can do only because demand is outstripping supply. This pricing power is a big part of why their stocks are reacting so strongly: higher ASPs plus strong volumes boost revenue and margins simultaneously.

items.title: Lal PathLabs delivered FY26 results in-line with guidance - revenue grew 12.25% (within 11-12% guidance) and EBITDA margin was 28.32% (within 27-28% g...
items.description_html:
Lal PathLabs delivered FY26 results in-line with guidance - revenue grew 12.25% (within 11-12% guidance) and EBITDA margin was 28.32% (within 27-28% guidance). Q4 showed strong momentum with 16.6% YoY revenue growth, accelerating from YTD trend, suggesting early positive signal for FY27. Cash conversion remains strong at 1.26x with FCF of ₹4,623 Cr.

However, Q4 margin compression of 148 bps and elevated capex (₹1,734 Cr vs ₹130-140 Cr guidance) warrant monitoring. Strategic acquisitions and network expansion position company for growth, though competitive intensity in collection centers remains a concern.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 12.25% YoY to ₹27,629 Cr, slightly exceeding guidance range of 11-12%

EBITDA margin was 28.32% for FY26, at higher end of guided range 27-28%

Q4 FY26 revenue grew 16.6% YoY, accelerating from YTD trend of 10.8% pre-Q4

Operating cash flow to PAT ratio strong at 1.26x, free cash flow ₹4,623 Cr

Company maintains debt-free balance sheet with strong liquidity position of ₹13,321 Cr

Normalized PAT grew 18.2% YoY, stripping out one-time items

Risk Factors

Q4 EBITDA margin contracted 148 bps YoY to 26.6% due to higher channel partner fees

Capex of ₹1,734 Cr significantly exceeded guidance of ₹130-140 Cr

Fees to collection centers grew 41.2% YoY in Q4 indicating competitive intensity

PAT declined 15.2% YoY in Q4 due to high base from deferred tax credit

items.title: Laurus Labs delivered strong FY26 results with 22.7% revenue growth and 710 bps EBITDA margin expansion to 26.1%. The company generated robust free ca...
items.description_html:
Laurus Labs delivered strong FY26 results with 22.7% revenue growth and 710 bps EBITDA margin expansion to 26.1%. The company generated robust free cash flow of ₹553.55 crores while executing ₹1,070 crores capex, resulting in significant balance sheet deleveraging (Net Debt/EBITDA from 2.42x to 1.28x).

However, standalone Q4 performance showed a 23.9% PAT decline, indicating subsidiary contributions are masking weakness in core Indian operations. Asset turnover remains low at 0.69x vs 1.1x target.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 22.7% YoY to ₹6,812.90 crores for FY26
EBITDA margin expanded by 710 bps YoY to 26.1% for
full year, Q4 margin at 28.27%

PAT grew 151.1% YoY to ₹889.85 crores, normalized growth 135.6% YoY excluding one-time gain

Operating cash flow was robust at ₹1,623.5 cr (1.82x of PAT)

Free cash flow turned positive at ₹553.55 crores vs negative ₹39.35 crores in FY25

Net Debt/EBITDA improved significantly from 2.42x to 1.28x

Capex of ₹1,070 cr was executed while generating positive FCF and deleveraging

Risk Factors

Standalone Q4 PAT declined 23.9% YoY vs consolidated growth of 20.5%, core operations
showing weakness

Asset turnover at 0.69x remains well below management target of 1.1x

Standalone Q4 revenue declined 4.2% YoY indicating weakness in Indian operations

Inventory build-up of ₹141 cr in Q4 may indicate demand softening or overstocking

items.title: Kajaria Ceramics delivered an exceptional Q4 with PAT growth of 262.6% YoY and EBITDA margin expansion of 785 bps to 19.18%, driven by strong operatin...
items.description_html:
Kajaria Ceramics delivered an exceptional Q4 with PAT growth of 262.6% YoY and EBITDA margin expansion of 785 bps to 19.18%, driven by strong operating leverage (revenue +12.4% YoY vs expenses +2.2% YoY).

The bathware turnaround is confirmed with EBIT up 247% YoY, and the new growth engine (Others segment) grew 20.3% YoY. Strong cash generation (FCF ₹536 Cr, up 92% YoY) supports the ₹297 Cr share buyback and increased dividend of ₹14/share.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 12.4% YoY in Q4 (₹1,373.35 Cr), recovering from flat growth in Q3

PAT surged 262.6% YoY to ₹156.56 Cr in Q4

EBITDA margin expanded 785 bps YoY to 19.18% in Q4

Operating cash flow was ₹663.67 Cr (OCF/PAT ratio 1.36x)

Free cash flow doubled to ₹536.33 Cr in FY26

Bathware segment turned around with EBIT of ₹44.43 Cr (up 247% YoY)

Total debt reduced by ₹45.62 Cr to ₹130.32 Cr

Capex was ₹127.34 Cr vs guidance of ~₹200 Cr (capex moderation)

Risk Factors

Exceptional items of ₹44.02 Cr in FY26 included fraud loss (₹19.81 Cr) and labour code provision (₹19.43 Cr)

Subsidiaries contributed 9.4% to revenue but only 5.9% to PAT, indicating slight drag on profitability

Finance costs increased 12.98% YoY to ₹22.63 Cr in FY26

Q4 saw inventory drawdown of ₹94.28 Cr which increases reported COGS

items.title: Laurus Labs – Concall Notes (Q4 FY26) Results Snapshot: FY26 Revenue: ₹6,813 Cr (+23% YoY) Q4 Revenue: ₹1,812 Cr (+5% YoY) Gross Margins: ~60–61% ...
items.description_html:
Laurus Labs – Concall Notes (Q4 FY26)

Results Snapshot:

FY26 Revenue: ₹6,813 Cr (+23% YoY)
Q4 Revenue: ₹1,812 Cr (+5% YoY)
Gross Margins: ~60–61% (improved due to better mix)
EBITDA: ₹1,826 Cr (+64% YoY)
EBITDA Margin: 26.8% (operating leverage kicking in)
PAT: ₹889 Cr (+148% YoY)
Q4 PAT: ₹279 Cr (+19% YoY)

Growth achieved despite macro challenges, supported by strong execution and mix improvement

Business Performance:

1)CDMO:
-Revenue contribution increasing structurally (13% in FY19 → ~31% in FY26)

Growth driven by:

-Late-stage commercial API supply
-Transition from development to commercialization
-Multiple late-stage programs expected to commercialize from FY27
-No concentration risk, diversified pipeline across customers

Bio segment growth supported by:

-Diversification into higher value products
-Strong pipeline visibility
-Fermentation scale-up underway
-Commercial stage products deliver higher EBITDA margins vs development phase

Affordable Medicines (Generics):

-Growth driven by ARV and oncology segments
-ARV business remains stable with long-term visibility (~₹2800 Cr range)
-Nov-ARV segments growing faster, improving mix

Capex & Capacity Expansion:

-Reactor volume crossed 8200 KL
-Planned capex of ~₹3000 Cr over next 2 years

Key expansions:

-Large greenfield manufacturing facility to be ready by March 2027
-Peptide manufacturing capacity expected by Q2 FY27
-Fermentation capacity ramp-up in progress
-KRKA JV Phase 1 nearing completion
-Majority capex focused on medium and large-scale manufacturing
-Capex is growth-led with demand visibility

Margins & Profitability

-EBITDA margins improved to ~26–27% from 24%

Improvement driven by:

-Gross margin expansion
-Better product mix
-Operating leverage
-Commercial business has structurally higher profitability
-Margins expected to sustain, with potential for further improvement as utilization increases

Balance Sheet & Investments:

-Debt/EBITDA at ~1.25x, increased due to ongoing capex cycle
-Debt may increase slightly in near term due to investments
-Asset turnover currently ~0.9x, expected to improve with scale-up
-Strong operating cash flows supporting capex

Expansion into:

-Peptides (including high-growth segments like weight loss)
-Biotechnology and material science
-Non-pharma fermentation applications (industrial chemicals, polymers)
-Strong positioning as a long-term partner for global pharma clients
-API and advanced intermediate focus provides longer-term revenue visibility

Management Outlook:
-Growth momentum expected to continue into next year
-Operating leverage expected to further play out
-CDMO pipeline remains strong with multiple commercialization triggers
-Non-ARV business expected to drive incremental growth
-Gross margins expected to sustain at current levels or improve
-Credit cycle remains stable with no visible stress in capacity utilization

Disclosure: Holding and Biased
Analyst - @Imprathamgupta

items.title: RRKABEL delivered strong FY26 results with 27.6% revenue growth and 58% PAT growth, driven by Wires & Cables exceeding its 18% CAGR target with 31% gr...
items.description_html:
RRKABEL delivered strong FY26 results with 27.6% revenue growth and 58% PAT growth, driven by Wires & Cables exceeding its 18% CAGR target with 31% growth. EBITDA margin improved 152 bps to 7.92%, meeting annual guidance.

However, working capital deteriorated significantly (inventories +75%, CFO -40%) and FMEG segment remains loss-making, missing breakeven target. Balance sheet remains strong with 0.09x debt-to-equity. The core W&C business shows strong execution while FMEG turnaround remains a work in progress.

Positive Factors

Revenue grew 33.65% YoY in Q4 and 27.62% for FY26, exceeding W&C growth target of 18% CAGR

PAT grew 30.06% YoY in Q4 and 57.96% for FY26, with normalized PAT up 62.5% YoY

EBITDA margin improved 152 bps YoY for FY26 to 7.92%, meeting margin guidance target

W&C EBIT margin improved 143 bps to 8.85% for FY26, on track with 100 bps annual improvement target

Debt-to-equity ratio very strong at 0.09x, company maintains strong balance sheet

ROE achieved 20.67%, meeting management's >20% target

Risk Factors

FMEG segment posted EBIT loss of ₹9.3 Cr in Q4, missing breakeven target for Q4 FY26

FMEG revenue grew only 3.1% vs target of 25% CAGR, significantly underperforming

Working capital deteriorated significantly: inventories up 75.2% YoY, receivables up 21.2%

Cash conversion weak: CFO/PAT ratio at 0.60x for FY26, CFO declined 40.3% YoY

Finance costs increased 61.3% YoY in Q4, indicating higher debt/working capital needs

Q4 W&C EBIT margin declined 27 bps YoY to 9.65% from 9.92%

items.title: PSP Projects met its FY26 revenue guidance (₹3,148.66 Crore within ₹3,100-3,200 Crore range) but significantly missed EBITDA margin guidance (6.00% ...
items.description_html:
PSP Projects met its FY26 revenue guidance (₹3,148.66 Crore within ₹3,100-3,200 Crore range) but significantly missed EBITDA margin guidance (6.00% vs 8-9% target). While Q4 showed strong execution with 65.7% YoY revenue growth and 226% PAT growth, full-year profitability declined 1.6% due to cost pressure.

Operating cash flow was exceptionally strong (5.81x PAT), but working capital deterioration (75% receivables growth) and margin compression remain concerns. Execution was in-line on revenue but poor on profitability.

Positive Factors

Revenue of ₹3,148.66 Crore met management's FY26 guidance of ₹3,100-3,200 Crore

Q4 revenue grew 65.74% YoY to ₹1,115.24 Crore with 37.21% QoQ acceleration

Q4 PAT grew 226.37% YoY to ₹21.09 Crore

Operating cash flow was strong at ₹322.82 Crore with 5.81x PAT conversion

Free cash flow positive at ₹129.85 Crore despite elevated capex

Capex of ₹192.96 Crore was in line with ~₹200 Crore guidance

Risk Factors

EBITDA margin of 6.00% missed 8-9% guidance by 200-300 bps

Trade receivables surged 75.2% YoY to ₹928.22 Crore, outpacing revenue growth

Other expenses surged 94.47% YoY to ₹81.58 Crore, driving margin compression

FY26 PAT declined 1.6% YoY to ₹55.52 Crore despite 25.3% revenue growth

Working capital stress evident from receivables growth vs revenue

items.title: RT menaka doshi: Pratyush Kumar, the 38-year old co-founder of India’s first homegrown AI company, Sarvam AI, was in the Bloomberg office yesterday f...
items.description_html:
RT menaka doshi
Pratyush Kumar, the 38-year old co-founder of India’s first homegrown AI company, Sarvam AI, was in the Bloomberg office yesterday for the Look Ahead 2026 event.

items.title: GHCLTEXTIL delivered in-line performance with FY26 revenue growth of 13.56% (meeting 14-15% guidance at low end) and EBITDA margin of 11.26% normalize...
items.description_html:
GHCLTEXTIL delivered in-line performance with FY26 revenue growth of 13.56% (meeting 14-15% guidance at low end) and EBITDA margin of 11.26% normalized (meeting 11% target). Q4 showed strong momentum with 28% YoY revenue growth and near-doubling of PAT.

However, significant cash flow deterioration (CFO/PAT at 0.06x) and working capital stress (inventories +42.7%, receivables +45.4%) funded by doubling debt raises concerns about earnings quality. The turnaround is confirmed with PBT up 47% YoY, but execution quality is good rather than superior given the cash conversion weakness.

Positive Factors

Q4 revenue grew 28.24% YoY to ₹363.69 Cr, showing strong recovery from H1 softness

Q4 PAT nearly doubled to ₹27.66 Cr, up 94.79% YoY

FY26 revenue of ₹1,318.60 Cr met guidance at the low end of 14-15% target

EBITDA margin expanded 116 bps YoY to 10.61% (normalized 11.26% met 11% target)

Operating leverage visible with employee costs and power costs growing slower than revenue

New capacities commissioned with ₹153.12 Cr moved from CWIP to PPE

Risk Factors

Operating cash flow collapsed to ₹4.55 Cr from ₹162.16 Cr in FY25, CFO/PAT ratio only 0.06x

Working capital stress: inventories up 42.7% and receivables up 45.4% vs revenue growth of 13.6%

Borrowings more than doubled to ₹134.22 Cr to fund working capital gap

One-time asset gain of ₹8.59 Cr inflated PAT; normalized PAT grew only 14.1% vs reported 25.7%

FY27 capex budget of ₹127.77 Cr signals continued investment but may further pressure cash

items.title: GOCOLORS reported a significant miss on its FY26 guidance with revenue declining 1.2% YoY (vs positive SSSG target) and PAT falling 36.7%. The company...
items.description_html:
GOCOLORS reported a significant miss on its FY26 guidance with revenue declining 1.2% YoY (vs positive SSSG target) and PAT falling 36.7%. The company achieved its gross margin guidance but missed on all growth and profitability metrics.

Operating deleverage from 4.6% expense growth against negative revenue, combined with 13.1% inventory buildup, indicates structural challenges. Strong cash generation and share buyback provide some offset, but execution quality is poor with guidance misses across multiple dimensions.

Positive Factors

Gross margin maintained at 63.2% within guided 62-63% range

Free cash flow strong at 12,554.75 Lakhs

Operating cash flow to PAT ratio healthy at 2.78x

Completed share buyback of 14,13,000 shares at ₹460

Zero financial debt on balance sheet

ASP grew 4.4% YoY in 9M FY26 exceeding 2-3% target

Risk Factors

Revenue declined 4.25% YoY in Q4 and 1.2% for full FY26

PAT declined 60% YoY in Q4 and 36.7% for FY26

EBITDA margin contracted 330 bps to 28.3%

Employee costs grew 11.1% YoY outpacing revenue

Inventories grew 13.1% YoY indicating working capital pressure

Store additions of 49 in 9M likely missed 80-90 target

Same-store sales growth negative at -4.2% YoY in Q4

The corridor traverses through six districts of Uttar Pradesh: Meerut, Hapur, Bulandshahar, Amroha, Sambhal and Budaun. Major structures include a 1 km long Ganga River Bridge, 9 major flyovers, 5 major bridges, 5 interchange flyovers, 14 minor bridges and 90 vehicular underpasses.

items.title: Extremely important commentary to keep in mind when studying and investing in Solar companies - "So, 140 GW of capacity we are talking about by FY28 a...
items.description_html:
Extremely important commentary to keep in mind when studying and investing in Solar companies -

"So, 140 GW of capacity we are talking about by FY28
and this includes a lot of new capacities, I mean people who have not have the experience of cells and lot of announcements that have made, and I think last time when we checked the publicly available data in May, that time it was about 12 to 15% of the capacities that were announced had no progress after even 3 years of announcement, because manufacturing cells is not easy, not only about the high capex but the complexity of the process and this we have seen that lot of different approaches are being taken." (Q3 concall snippet from Emvee Photovoltaic concall; no reco)

Solar cell manufacturing is essentially semiconductor fabrication. You are engineering at the atomic or molecular level — doping silicon with precise concentrations of boron or phosphorus, creating p-n junctions that are nanometers thin. Any contamination, temperature deviation, or process drift kills efficiency. This is closer to making chips than assembling panels.

Cell manufacturing involves ~15–20 distinct process steps:

Each step has tight process windows. A mistake at step 4 doesn't show up until step 18. Yield losses compound across steps — if each step has 98% yield, your overall yield across 15 steps is ~74%. Improving yield by even 1% per step is a major competitive advantage.

This is extremely important. Whenever you're studying solar players who are getting into cells, look at the execution and tech know-how!

The module business is commoditizing rapidly — the real value in the solar supply chain is increasingly at the cell level, which is exactly why everyone wants to be there, and exactly why most won't succeed.

items.title: Picture this: It's 2002, and a 43-year-old IIT-IIM alumnus sits in his Delhi office, staring at a spreadsheet that would make most hospitality executi...
items.description_html:
Picture this: It's 2002, and a 43-year-old IIT-IIM alumnus sits in his Delhi office, staring at a spreadsheet that would make most hospitality executives laugh. The data shows something absurd—in India, there were only 25,000 branded hotel rooms, of which 20,000 were at 5-star properties. An inverted pyramid in a country of over a billion people. While luxury hotels catered to the elite few, and unorganized guest houses served the masses, there was virtually nothing in between.

That was the Thesis for Lemon Tree!

Patu Keswani founded Lemon Tree Hotels in 2002 and opened its first hotel with 49 rooms in May 2004. Fast forward to today: the company now owns and operates 130 hotels with a total of 11,772 rooms in 80 locations across India, the UAE, Bhutan, and Nepal. Today's market cap stands at ₹9400 crore, making it one of India's most valuable hospitality companies.

Keswani's engineering background kicked in. He began deconstructing the hotel P&L like a machine, identifying every component that drove costs. Room size? Indians didn't need 400 square feet; 180 would do. Marble lobbies? Skip them. Twenty-four-hour coffee shops? Unnecessary. But air conditioning, clean bathrooms, comfortable beds, and reliable Wi-Fi? Non-negotiable.

Anticipating the enormous yet latent demand for mid-market and economy hotels in India, Patu Keswani promoted Lemon Tree Hotels in late 2002. This wasn't a business plan—it was a mathematical proof that an entire segment of the market was being ignored. The inverted pyramid wasn't just a market inefficiency; it was Keswani's opportunity to build a category that didn't exist.

Src – Empor top, no reco

1. Current-Chair Powell indicated that he is going to stay on as governor "for a period of time" even after his Chairmanship expires in the middle of May. items.description_html:
No, the Iranian Oil Industry Isn’t About to 'Explode', writes @JavierBlas

"The economic blow would be immense as the country loses crucial oil revenue. But I don’t buy the argument that its oil wells would suffer irremediable damage — and neither do most experts with on-the-ground experience in the petroleum sector. Sadly, the US administration appear to be relying on flawed analysis, often amplified in social media and derived from Claude, ChatGPT and other artificial intelligence models. The state-owned National Iranian Oil Company has plenty of experience throttling production up and down; a stoppage lasting weeks, or even a couple of months, would not be enough to condemn the wells."

Read 👇

items.title: In my view, affluent peoples inflation is closer to 6% a year on average. I think 12% a year is overstated as an inflation goal, even if I looked at m...
items.description_html:
In my view, affluent peoples inflation is closer to 6% a year on average. I think 12% a year is overstated as an inflation goal, even if I looked at my own spending from 2006 to now.

Most people's household expenses actually reduce when their kids grow up, because they keep higher amounts in kid specific goals. In general, as we speak with more and more people, the majority seem to be very well grounded in terms of not overspending for lifestyle inflation.

Twitter often overstates inflation because there will be someone who paid Rs 300 for a mango or whose kids go to a more expensive school. But it is unreasonable to plan for extreme inflation, and will just lead to disappointment especially if you're not the crazy spemder type.

More money is welcome of course. But let's be reasonable in our goal estimates. It leaves us in a better capacity to spend and enjoy life today.

items.title: The story of Maruti 800 - The Maruti 800 was priced at Rs 47,500 (roughly equivalent to Rs 5 lakhs in today's money), wasn't just competitively priced...
items.description_html:
The story of Maruti 800 -

The Maruti 800 was priced at Rs 47,500 (roughly equivalent to Rs 5 lakhs in today's money), wasn't just competitively priced—it was revolutionary.

The Ambassador cost nearly twice as much, consumed far more fuel, and came with waiting periods that stretched into years. The Maruti 800 promised something almost unbelievable: immediate delivery, Japanese reliability, and a claimed mileage of 25.95 kilometers per liter that seemed almost magical in an era when fuel efficiency was becoming increasingly critical due to oil price shocks.

The market response was nothing short of phenomenal. When Maruti Udyog opened bookings in April 1983, they expected interest but weren't prepared for the tsunami of demand that followed. Within just three months, the company received bookings for more than 135,000 units—this in a country where the entire annual car production was less than 40,000 units.

The booking amount itself became a talking point in middle-class households, with families pooling resources, selling ancestral jewelry, and breaking fixed deposits to secure their place in the queue for this revolutionary vehicle.

Src – Empor top, no reco

“We’ll either make this work…or we’ll all be fired.”

That was the bet behind Blinkit.

From being written off as a drag on Zomato to becoming its biggest growth engine—this is a story of conviction and contrarian bets.

In a conversation with Shereen Bhan on Young Turks Reloaded, Eternal Group CEO Albinder Dhindsa breaks down what most people get wrong about quick commerce—why it’s not just speed, why it’s deeply tech-led, and why India’s consumers are far ahead of the curve.

From tier 2 adoption to navigating a low-trust system—and building at scale despite it, this isn’t just a startup story. It’s a masterclass in building in India.

Bloom Energy $BE just delivered a knockout Q1 beat estimates and raised FY26 revenue guidance to $3.4B–$3.8B.

That's ~80% YoY growth

Gross margins trending toward ~34%, adj. EBITDA guided at $650M–$800M, and operating cash flow crossing $ 200M.

On top of it Oracle picked Bloom as the sole power provider for Project Jupiter, one of its marquee AI data center builds replacing gas turbines and diesel generators entirely.

Q2 revenue guided to be at least as strong as Q1. AI infrastructure needs reliable, dense power. Bloom is positioning itself right at that bottleneck.

Disclaimer - not a recommendation to buy/sell

This cover the other 80% by thinking portfolio over stocks (sizing, timing, behavior control, fear & greed, reduce/selling) based on case study led interviews with multiple successful fund managers worldwide. Many of them may not be household names. Good case studies on what worked for them. Better suited for serious investors and practioners.

items.title: RT Natasha Malpani 👁: have we ever had this level of consensus and concentration at the frontier? the uncertainty hasn’t decreased. the ambiguity ...
items.description_html:
RT Natasha Malpani 👁
have we ever had this level of consensus and concentration at the frontier?

the uncertainty hasn’t decreased. the ambiguity about which AI architectures actually work, which applications retain margin, which distribution channels survive agents is not resolved.

what has changed is that enough capital has been committed that dissenting looks costly.

perception shapes reality. but concentration of capital and narrative into the same categories at the same moment means the wins get competed away and the losses get amplified.

the frontier companies that actually breakout will not look like what we predict today.


Linas Beliūnas: In 2026, for the first time in recent memory, every major VC firm is saying the same thing.

YC wants AI agents that replace service workers. So does a16z.

Sequoia wants stablecoins as financial infrastructure. So does YC.

Everyone wants AI applied to the physical world -

items.title: March 15, 2019 - The roadshow for Polycab's IPO had reached Mumbai, the final stop after presentations in Singapore, London, New York, and Hong Kong. ...
items.description_html:
March 15, 2019 - The roadshow for Polycab's IPO had reached Mumbai, the final stop after presentations in Singapore, London, New York, and Hong Kong. The audience—fund managers controlling over ₹2 lakh crores—had heard hundreds of IPO pitches. Most were forgettable. Inder Jaisinghani intended to ensure Polycab wasn't.

He began not with financial projections or market opportunities, but with a photograph: his father's original electrical shop in Lohar Chawl, barely visible through monsoon flooding, the inventory floating in muddy water. "Forty-three years ago, we lost everything in one night. The next morning, we started again. That resilience—not our factories or products—is what you're investing in." The room's energy shifted. This wasn't another PowerPoint recitation; it was a founder's manifesto.

The IPO timing seemed questionable to many observers. Election uncertainty loomed with polls just weeks away. The IL&FS crisis had frozen credit markets. Jet Airways had just collapsed, spooking investors about corporate governance. Yet Kotak Mahindra Capital, Axis Capital, and Citigroup—the investment banks managing the offering—saw opportunity in adversity. The very factors deterring others created scarcity value for quality companies willing to brave uncertain markets.

Amazing way to pitch an IPO indeed! Loved this

Src – empor top , no reco

items.description_html:
Looks like another big deal is brewing in the consumer brands space.

Wishcare, a seven-year-old company known for its hair and skincare products, is looking for a buyer at a Rs 3,000-3,500 crore valuation.

This represents over a 20x jump from its last valuation of Rs 141 crore in late 2023, when Unilever Ventures led a Rs 20 crore round - it hasn’t raised any other funding since.

In FY24, it's revenues stood at Rs 70 crore.

In FY25, revenues grew 187% to Rs 202 crore, with a net profit of Rs 51 crore - a massive margin.

The company has reached Rs 350 crore in revenue and Rs 100 crore in EBITDA in FY26, according to the report.

items.title: RT Nimesh Shah: CLSA on Bandhan BK O-P, TP raised to Rs 220 2nd good quarter for Bandhan Bank after several tough quarters since mid-2024. 4QFY26 PAT ...
items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
CLSA on Bandhan BK
O-P, TP raised to Rs 220
2nd good quarter for Bandhan Bank after several tough quarters since mid-2024.
4QFY26 PAT was 26% above estimate, driven by stronger fees and lower credit costs, partly offset by higher opex
Bank hit a 1% ROA after six quarters.
AUM growth picked up to 13% YoY, aided by a pickup in MFI growth.
Reported NIM improved 30bp QoQ to 6.2%, driven largely by term deposit repricing.
But, most importantly, asset quality improved meaningfully
Net slippage ratio improved 100bp QoQ to 1.9% and was half of the 2QFY26 level; this drove the sharp credit cost beat
Going ahead, management targets 14-15% loan growth with a largely stable loan mix.
It also expects another 10-20bp NIM improvement and a pickup in fees.

Jefferies on Bandhan Bk
Buy, TP Rs 215
For Mar-Qtr, Bandhan Bank's profit of Rs5bn (up 68% YoY) beat estimates with lower credit costs & higher fees.
Stability in MFI sector & trends in election-bound states are supporting credit quality.
This, along with scope for expansion in NIM should aid turnaround in earnings over FY27-28
Tweak est. to factor 4Q results & are tad conservative vs mtg guidance given volatility in this sector

UBS on Bandhan BK
Neutral, TP raised to Rs 200
Q4 PAT beat: Strong beat on credit costs; partly offset by higher Opex
MFI book grows at c7.5% QoQ; CASA ratio up c200bp
Management aim for a FY27 exit ROA of 1.6% - 1.8%
Believe stock's current valuation prices in a large part of asset quality improvement & see limited upside from current levels (c1x FY27E P/BV, stock up 24% YTD)

Macquarie on Bandhan BK
U-P, TP Rs 130
PAT beats estimates driven by lower credit costs
Slippages moderate, loan growth picks up pace
Lower borrowing costs support NIM expansion
Management guidance remains ambitious, targeting 14– 15% loan growth & 1.6–1.8% RoA by 4QFY27, supported by 10–20bps NIM expansion (from deposit repricing), fee income traction & lower PSLC costs.
4Q stood out for Bandhan in an otherwise weak year, but believe consistent performance is needed for a possible re-rating

JPM on Bandhan BK
Neutral, TP raised to Rs 157
4QFY26: Core performance misses, AQ improvement supporting RoA expansion, sustainability key
While Bandhan’s strategic pivot is underway, & guidance for 1.6-1.7% of RoA by FY27 exit (vs 1.1% in 4QFY26, reported) is positive, trend has been very volatile historically
Await more visibility on sustainability of ongoing recovery, especially in light of ongoing macroeconomic headwinds.
Current valuations at 1.03x FY27 P/BV appear fair & cap any significant upside
Raise forecasts for FY27/28E by 17%/2% resp.

@CNBCTV18News @_RituSingh
